coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial effects of carbon quantum dots@hematite nanostructures deposited on titanium against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Moradlou, Omran
Rabiei, Zeinab
Delavari, Niusha
coredata.dc:description: Thin films of nanostructured hematite (α-Fe2O3) and carbon quantum dots-incorporated hematite (CQDs@α-Fe2O3) were uniformly grown on titanium substrate and their antibacterial properties against Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria were studied under dark and illumination conditions. The surface morphology of the samples was investigated with FE-SEM and HRTEM. The antimicrobial investigations demonstrate that α-Fe2O3 and CQDs@α-Fe2O3 are toxic to the selected microorganisms and the samples exhibit sustainable antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacterial strains compared to Gram-negative bacteria mainly due to the presence of extra outer membrane layer in Gram negative bacteria. Based on the results of the antibacterial activity of α-Fe2O3 and CQDs@α-Fe2O3 under dark and the light irradiation conditions, it was concluded that the preferred mechanism of bactericidal activity of hematite-based thin films is both the penetration of iron cations into the bacteria cell via their membrane and the generation of reactive oxygen species. These results indicate that CQDs@α-Fe2O3 nanoparticulates provide insight into the development of visible-light antimicrobial materials for their potential bactericidal applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium
Hematite
Carbon quantum dots
Antibacterial activity
Thin film

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bactericidal effects of samples against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria under dark conditions
Photoinactivation of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images from the surface of Ti/α-Fe2O3 (a) and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples prepared by hydrothermal method under the optimized experimental conditions. HRTEM images of CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample (c and d).

The bactericidal activity of the prepared Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples against S. aureus and E. coli bacteria strains under dark conditions. The initial concentrations of bacteria strain (CFU/mL) have been indicated in Table 1.
Zone of inhibition produced by samples Ti/α-Fe2O3 (a,b) and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 (c,d) against E. coli (a,c) and S. aureus (b,d) bacterial strains.

Antibacterial activity of Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples against S. aureus and E. coli bacteria strains under visible light irradiation. The initial concentrations of bacteria strain (CFU/mL) have been indicated in Table 1.
The E. coli and S. aureus bacteria inactivation kinetics (with an initial concentration of 1.5 × 106 CFU/mL, pH 7.0) by Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample under visible light irradiation.
The FE-SEM images of native E. coli (a) and S. aureus (b) and treated E. coli (c) and S. aureus (d) with Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample under visible light illumination conditions.
Antibacterial activity results of Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples under dark and visible light illumination conditions.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial effects of carbon quantum dots@hematite nanostructures deposited on titanium against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Physics & Chemistry, Alzahra University, P.O. Box: 1993893973, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thin films of nanostructured hematite (α-Fe2O3) and carbon quantum dots-incorporated hematite (CQDs@α-Fe2O3) were uniformly grown on titanium substrate and their antibacterial properties against Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria were studied under dark and illumination conditions. The surface morphology of the samples was investigated with FE-SEM and HRTEM. The antimicrobial investigations demonstrate that α-Fe2O3 and CQDs@α-Fe2O3 are toxic to the selected microorganisms and the samples exhibit sustainable antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacterial strains compared to Gram-negative bacteria mainly due to the presence of extra outer membrane layer in Gram negative bacteria. Based on the results of the antibacterial activity of α-Fe2O3 and CQDs@α-Fe2O3 under dark and the light irradiation conditions, it was concluded that the preferred mechanism of bactericidal activity of hematite-based thin films is both the penetration of iron cations into the bacteria cell via their membrane and the generation of reactive oxygen species. These results indicate that CQDs@α-Fe2O3 nanoparticulates provide insight into the development of visible-light antimicrobial materials for their potential bactericidal applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing bacterial resistance through evolutionary processes towards conventional antibiotics such as penicillin and amoxicillin is a major global health concern in current era [1,2]. These antibiotics are chemically modified natural compounds including β-lactams and are the backbone to combat contagious diseases. Although these antibiotics have transformed modern medicine and saved millions of lives [2], however, the broad use and abuse have resulted in emergence of bacterial resistance and adverse side effects [3]. So, the treatment of bacterial infections utilizing these classical antibiotics is certainly becoming more serious global problem [1,4].

In this regard, among all the new developed strategies, the use of nanostructured metal oxides can be emerged as a promising alternative with very distinct pathways of bactericidal action [5–8]. In fact, owing to their multiple mechanisms of bactericidal action, metal oxides would serve as a new class of effective disinfectants with different mechanism compared to the conventional antibiotics. In addition, besides to their use as antimicrobial compounds, they may have diverse applications in food packaging as well as the coating agents on medical devices and implants to make them resistant against microbial infections [3]. Titanium-based alloys are biocompatible and are finding ever-increasing applications as biomaterials in orthopedic implant materials and devices due to their excellent mechanical and biological performance [9]. However, some bacterial strains have the potential to produce slime which facilitates the adhesion of produced biofilms on orthopedic implant devices which enhances the bacterial resistance by preventing antibiotic action. So, a tightly deposited biocompatible thin film with bactericidal action on the surface of medical devices and implantable materials would exhibit reduced microbial adhesion in vitro and inhibit the infection that is the main cause of dental implant failure [5].

Metal oxides such as TiO2 [10–15], ZnO [16–19], CuO and Cu2O [8,20,21], Bi4O5Br2 [22] and MgO [23,24] can serve as effective disinfectants due to their efficient antibacterial activity and relatively non-toxic profile. So, there is a growing interest in the investigation of the bactericidal action of these photoactive semiconductors under the light illumination. The mechanism of the bacteria killing under the irradiation of semiconductor-based materials is mainly the photogeneration of electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs at the surface of illuminated sample. The electrons in the conduction band of the semiconductor can react with molecular oxygen and produce superoxide anion (O2 •-) through a reductive process, and holes in the valence band can oxidize water to generate reactive hydroxyl radicals (
OH) [25,26]. However, the disadvantage of using TiO2 and ZnO is their wide bandgap that UV light is required to activate the photocatalyst and initiate the killing of the bacteria. So, from this point of view, the investigation of the antibacterial activity of the visible light active materials such as hematite is important.

Among the semiconducting metal oxides, iron oxides including hematite (α-Fe2O3) [3,27–30], maghemite (γ-Fe2O3) [31] and magnetite (Fe3O4) [27] exhibit efficient bactericidal properties over a wide spectrum of bacteria. Interestingly, iron oxide-based compounds are biocompatible and have minimal or no cytotoxicity to human cells [3,32,33]. So, they can locally kill bacteria or slow down their growth, without being in general toxic to surrounding tissue. Although the antibacterial activity of maghemite and magnetite have been widely studied, the bactericidal action of hematite is still under investigation. Hematite has been identified as an excellent semiconductor material for its potential applications in photoelectrochemical water splitting processes [34–36] and solar cells [37,38] as well as targeted drug delivery and biomedical applications [39,40] because of its good chemical stability, low cost and environmental friendliness. Interestingly, it was reported that hematite is non-toxic to beneficial soil microbes and does not cause lysis of red blood cells [41]. Finally, hematite have already been approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for food and medical applications [3,27,28]. So, it seems that there is still a sustained interest in the development of antibacterial activity of hematite-based materials under dark and the illumination conditions. Carbon nanomaterials such as graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing attention because of their antimicrobial properties [42–44]. CQDs can destroy the cell structure of bacterial strains [42].

In this work, regarding the above-mentioned issues on α-Fe2O3 and CQDs and based on our recent works on quantum sized carbon materials and the interesting results in photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical processes [35] as well as Fenton-like reactions [45], we selected CQDs to examine the antibacterial activity of CQDs@α-Fe2O3. So, the bactericidal properties of hematite (α-Fe2O3) and carbon quantum dots-incorporated hematite (CQDs@α-Fe2O3) against Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria are studied under dark and illumination conditions. Based on the obtained results, Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQDs@α-Fe2O3 samples exhibit sustainable antibacterial activity against S. aureus strains compared to E. coli. So, it is expected that the hematite-based nanomaterials may provide insight into the development of visible-light antimicrobial materials for their potential applications in medical devices.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and ultrapure deionized water (DI water, 18 MΩ) was used throughout the work. For the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an electrochemical method was used as reported elsewhere [46] by using two graphite rods as anode and cathode. Briefly, the graphite electrodes were immersed in a 100 ml solution of ethanol:H2O (99:1) containing 0.30 g of sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and a constant current intensity of 180 mA cm−2 was applied for 24 h. The obtained soluble CQDs were then separated by silica-gel column chromatography after the treatment with MgSO4 [46].

Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples were fabricated by the hydrothermal method as reported in our previous work [35]. In brief, for the fabrication of Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples, a pre-cleaned Ti sheet (1 × 3cm2) was immersed vertically into the highly cleaned 50-mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave containing 0.1 mg mL−1 CQD, Fe3+ (0.05 M) and NaNO3 (1.0 M). pH of the solution was adjusted to 1.40 by adding HCl, and the vessel was heated to 100 °C for 6 h in an oven. Then, the sample was annealed at 390 °C for 2 h. For the fabrication of Ti/α-Fe2O3, the same procedure was used where the CQD solution was replaced with DI water.

The antibacterial activities of the Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 6538, PTCC 1112) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922, PTCC 1399) bacteria were studied under dark and illumination conditions by using an antibacterial drop test. Dimension of all the samples utilized in this work was 1 × 3 cm2. The glassware and samples were sterilized at 120 °C for 15 min before the microbiological tests. Culture of the bacteria was carried out on a nutrient agar plate at 37 °C for 24 h. The appropriate amount of cultured bacteria with the concentration of 1.5 × 108 colony forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL) were added into the 10 mL of saline solution to reach their concentration to about 1 × 106 CFU/mL. The exact initial concentration of the bacteria (CFU/mL) has been indicated in each experiment. The pH value of the solution was 7.0. Then, 100 μL of saline solution containing bacteria was spread on the surface of each sample in a sterilized Petri dish in incubator. After 24 h, the bacteria were transferred to a nutrient agar plate and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h for counting the surviving bacterial colonies using an optical microscope. The tests were also examined under the illumination conditions. After the spread of saline solution containing bacteria on the surface of sample, the sample was irradiated by a 30 mW cm−2 tungsten lamp at room temperature in incubator. Finally, after 24 h light irradiation, the bacteria were transferred to a phosphate buffer saline solution and the bacterial suspension was spread on a nutrient agar plate and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h for counting the surviving bacterial colonies. The reported data were the mean values of three distinct experiments. The uncoated Ti sheet was used as a control sample in all the antibacterial tests.

The morphology of the samples was observed with field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, EM3900 M, ZEISS, Germany, and FE-SEM, Tescan, Mira3) equipped with EDAX energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) for inspecting the elemental composition of the samples. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Hitachi H-9500) was used for size and morphology studies. To study the crystal phase of samples, X-ray diffraction (PW1800, Philips) with CoKα radiation (λ = 1.78 Å) was used.

Morphology of the Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples were investigated by FE-SEM and HRTEM. Fig. 1 a and b show the FE-SEM images of α-Fe2O3 and CQD@α-Fe2O3 deposited on Ti substrate, respectively. As it is clear, the morphology of synthesized α-Fe2O3 is nanorod with the average diameter of about 25 nm, while the morphology of CQD@α-Fe2O3 is nanoparticle, which the HRTEM image confirm (Fig. 1 c and d). As we have reported recently in our previous work [35], in the synthesis of CQD@α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, CQDs act as nano-scaffolds for the growth of CQD@α-Fe2O3 nanoparticulates as core@shell nanostructures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bactericidal effects of samples against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria under dark conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The bactericidal activity of the prepared Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples was assessed against two pathogenic bacterial strains including Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli bacteria under dark conditions. As Fig. 2 and Table 1 shows, the bactericidal activity of Ti/α-Fe2O3 against E. coli was low (˜10%). In contrast, about 65% of the S. aureus bacteria was inactivated by Ti/α-Fe2O3 sample. By using Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample, about 20% of E. coli and 70% of S. aureus bacteria were inactivated under dark conditions. In fact, both Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples have higher tendency to inactivate S. aureus compared to E. coli. It means that the S. aureus bacterial strains are more sensitive to antibacterial metal oxide compounds compared to E. coli. These result are in agreement with previous reports [8,27]. The higher resistance of Gram negative strain against hematite-based compounds and more antibacterial activity of the samples against Gram positive bacterial strains is mainly due to the presence of extra outer membrane layer in Gram negative bacteria [27]. So, hematite has more prominent toxicity against Gram positive bacterial strains and the Gram negative strain has higher resistance against hematite.

The antibacterial activity of Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples against bacterial strain in dark conditions can be assigned to the release of Fe3+ ions from α-Fe2O3 or CQD@α-Fe2O3 and their interaction with cytoplasmic water inside bacterial cell (called Fenton reaction) and then, the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as superoxide O2 •− and hydroxyl (
OH) radicals [3,47]. ROS can penetrate to the cell membrane of bacteria and damage their proteins and DNA, and ultimately results in death of bacteria [48,49]. Fig. 3 shows the zone of inhibition produced by samples Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 against E. coli and S. aureus bacterial strains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoinactivation of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the photoinactivation of E. coli and S. aureus bacterial strains on the surface of Ti/α-Fe2O3 and Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples, the visible light photocatalytic activity of the samples was investigated. Because the energy band gap of both samples is ˜2.1 eV [35], they were illuminated by a tungsten lamp as visible light source. When the samples are illuminated, the electron-hole pairs (e−-h+) are generated at the surface of semiconductor. So, the electrons with high reducing power in the conduction band can react with molecular oxygen to produce reactive superoxide anion radicals (O2
-), and the holes in the valence band of semiconductor can abstract electrons from water to generate reactive hydroxyl radicals (
OH) through an oxidative process [25]. In fact, under the light illumination, it is expected that the higher amounts of ROS would be generated and consequently, lower percent of the bacteria would be survived. The results (Fig. 4 and Table 1) showed that the inactivation of E. coli with Ti/α-Fe2O3 under the light illumination is about 50% higher than over dark conditions. Also, the photoinactivation of E. coli with Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample under the light illumination is higher compared to dark conditions (Table 1) most likely due to the generated e−-h+ pairs on Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3, resulting more generated ROS on Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample.

As we reported recently in our previous work [35], under the light illumination, CQDs as conductive nano-scaffold have the pivotal role in CQD@α-Fe2O3 to overcome the short hole-diffusion length and poor hole mobility limitations of hematite. So, higher amounts of holes can diffuse toward the surface of Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample and consequently, higher amounts of reactive
OH radicals will be produced at the surface of Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample. In fact, based on these studies, the probable mechanism is the h+ transfer (or h+ hopping) from valence band (VB) of α-Fe2O3 to the HOMO of CQDs. Fig. 5 shows the bacteria inactivation kinetics by Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample under visible light irradiation. Compared to the previously reported iron oxide based compounds [26,30], the kinetics of inactivation is slower, indicating lower amounts of ROS generation at the sample surface probably due to the low diffusion length of hole produced at the illuminated CQD@α-Fe2O3 compound.

The FE-SEM images of native and treated S. aureus and E. coli bacteria strains with Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample are shown in Fig. 6 . As it is clear, the cell membranes of both bacteria were significantly disrupted and subsequently, the intracellular contents of bacteria leaked outside. The chemical interactions between the iron cations and bacteria may be the primary reason for disruption of the integrity of bacteria via decomposition of functional groups in the proteins and lipopolysaccharides of their outer membranes [50,51]. To understand the mechanism, the concentration of Fe3+ after 24 h experiment of E coli inactivation was measured and obtained to be 0.4 ± 0.1 mg L−1 (n = 3), indicating the Fe-leaching from Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample to the test solution. This result is in agreement with the previously reported works indicating the metal ion release during the inactivation process [12,26,30,51]. The electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged E. coli and Fe3+ ions [30], causing the destruction of the bacteria. The iron cations can penetrate into the bacteria cell via their membrane. So, it can be concluded that the prevailing mechanism is the diffusion of Fe3+ ions inside the bacteria and producing ROS radicals. To get more insights into the effect of ROS species in bacteria inactivation, the inactivation process of E. coli with Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 sample was performed in the presence of •OH radical scavenger, tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) [45]. As a result, in this condition, E. coli was inactivated 15 ± 5% (n = 3) instead of 35%, indicating the role of
OH radicals in bacteria inactivation. So, as the second mechanism, ROS can be formed under the light illumination conditions and penetrate to the cell membrane of the bacteria and damage them. It is implied from the FE-SEM images (Fig. 6c and d) that S. aureus bacteria can be easily deformed in interaction with antibacterial agents and after the damage of membrane, the cell contents leak outside. However, for E. coli bacteria strain, it can be concluded that their cell walls are more resistive against oxidative species. In fact, the cell walls of Gram-negative bacteria are more complex and contain an extra outer membrane which often confers resistance to chemicals [51].

To investigate the stability of Ti/CQD@α-Fe2O3 samples in antibacterial tests, the experiments were performed for three cycles. Based on the obtained results, E. coli and S. aureus were inactivated 31 ± 6% and 78 ± 5%, respectively, indicating that the sample had almost its bactericidal activity and the bacterial inactivation kinetics are not slow down. These obtained results indicate that the fabricated samples are promising candidates for antibacterial applications in food packaging and orthopedic implants.

Herein, thin films of hematite and carbon quantum dots-incorporated hematite were tightly deposited on titanium substrate and their antibacterial properties against S. aureus and E. coli bacteria were studied under dark and illumination conditions. The obtained results demonstrate that CQDs@α-Fe2O3 exhibits sustainable antibacterial activity against S. aureus bacterial strains compared to E. coli mainly due to the presence of extra outer membrane layer in E. coli as the FE-SEM studies after the treatment of bacteria with CQDs@α-Fe2O3 confirmed. Based on the results of the antibacterial activity of α-Fe2O3 and CQDs@α-Fe2O3 under dark and the light irradiation conditions, it can be concluded that the preferred mechanism of bactericidal activity of hematite-based thin films is both the penetration of iron cations into the bacteria cell via their membrane and the generation of reactive oxygen species via Fenton reaction. Finally, the stability of the samples was tested and found that they had almost their bactericidal activity for at least three times. So, it is expected that the hematite-based nanomaterials can provide insight into the development of visible-light antimicrobial materials for their potential applications in medical devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Research Council of Alzahra University for financial support. A special thanks go to Dr. Juliusz Warzywoda and Dr. Alireza Banazadeh (Texas Tech University, United States) for HRTEM image analysis of the samples. We are also grateful to Dr. Z. Ziaei for her assistance with antibacterial activity of the samples.
